feedback.text: I respectfully urge you to consider expanding the scope of the Digital Services Act (DSA) to address a growing concern among consumers and preservationists regarding the long-term accessibility of video games. Specifically, I propose that the DSA include provisions to prohibit video game publishers from permanently disabling access to games that have been legally purchased by consumers. In recent years, there has been a disturbing trend where publishers shut down servers or remove critical functionality, rendering games entirely unplayableeven in single-player modesafter official support ends. This practice undermines consumer rights, digital ownership, and the preservation of cultural content. To counter this, video game developers and publishers should be required to provide a clear and transparent end-of-life plan for every game. This plan should ensure that, once official support is withdrawn, the game can still be accessed and played in some formwhether through server emulation, offline modes, or the release of necessary code or tools to the public under reasonable conditions. This issue is central to the "Stop Destroying Videogames" initiative, registered as European Citizens Initiative (ECI) 2024/000007. The initiative calls for stronger protections to preserve video games as cultural and artistic works, ensuring they are not arbitrarily removed or rendered inaccessible by their publishers after sale. Preserving video games is not just a matter of entertainmentit is a matter of cultural heritage, consumer rights, and digital sustainability. As more creative works shift into digital-only formats, it is essential for EU legislation to ensure that these works do not disappear simply because support has ended or business decisions have changed. Thank you for your attention to this matter. I hope that you will consider integrating these principles into future revisions or extensions of the Digital Services Act.
